FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from ABCD Study COVID-19 Rapid Response Research Release, May 2020 survey; 5412 ABCD participants ages 12-13.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD cross-sectional data; reports multiple linear regression associations between mental health/resiliency factors and total screen use.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in JAMA Pediatrics.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 identifies composite screen time (multiplayer gaming, single-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, browsing internet, streaming/watching) paired with individual-trait outcomes (mental health, stress, social support, coping). Step 2: composite includes TV/movies and generic gaming (unspecified platform) without separate per-modality breakout; fails composite-content check per binding rule.

notes: Paper reports associations between mental health/resiliency and total screen use, but composite includes non-phone/non-SM items (TV, generic gaming) without isolation.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study COVID-19 survey, May 2020; N=5335 parent-adolescent dyads, ages 10-14.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; reports parent vs. adolescent reports of recreational screen time and sociodemographic predictors of discrepancies.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Academic Pediatrics.

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures and compares parent and adolescent reports of recreational screen time (multiplayer gaming, single-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, internet browsing, streaming) but reports NO association between screen time and any individual-trait-level outcome. Analysis is purely methodological (parent vs. adolescent concordance); no outcome variable is measured.

notes: Measurement agreement study; no outcome variable analyzed. Focus is on concordance between parent and adolescent reports during COVID-19.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study Year 2 (2018-2020), N=9429; ages predominantly 11-12 years.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD data; multiple logistic regression of sociodemographic factors and screen time modalities with cyberbullying victimization and perpetration.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Academic Pediatrics.

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper analyzes total screen time (and component modalities) with cyberbullying victimization/perpetration. Step 1: Total screen time composite includes TV/movies, single/multiplayer gaming (platform unspecified), texting, social media, video chat, internet. Step 2: Composite bundles TV, generic console/PC gaming, and phone/SM items without per-device breakout; fails composite-content check per binding rule: multi-device aggregated time fails IC2 unless device-specific breakout exists.

notes: Paper reports associations between total/component screen time and cyberbullying, but primary analysis uses composite including TV and unspecified gaming without modality isolation.
